The **Ottawa Senators** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Sénateurs d'Ottawa*), officially the **Ottawa Senators Hockey Club**[[b]](#cite_note-6) and colloquially known as the **Sens**, are a professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team based in [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"). The Senators compete in the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) as a member of the [Atlantic Division](/wiki/Atlantic_Division_(NHL) "Atlantic Division (NHL)") in the [Eastern Conference](/wiki/Eastern_Conference_(NHL) "Eastern Conference (NHL)"). The team plays its home games at [Canadian Tire Centre](/wiki/Canadian_Tire_Centre "Canadian Tire Centre"), which opened in 1996.[[5]](#cite_note-7)

Founded and established by Ottawa real estate developer [Bruce Firestone](/wiki/Bruce_Firestone "Bruce Firestone"), the team is the second NHL franchise to use the Ottawa Senators name. The [original Ottawa Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators_(original) "Ottawa Senators (original)"), founded in 1883, won the [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") 11 times,[[c]](#cite_note-8) playing in the NHL from 1917 until 1934. On December 6, 1990, after a two-year public campaign by Firestone, the NHL awarded a new franchise, which began play in the [1992–93 season](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_NHL_season "1992–93 NHL season").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFinnigan1992201-9) The Senators have made 17 playoff appearances, won four division titles, and won the 2003 [Presidents' Trophy](/wiki/Presidents%27_Trophy "Presidents' Trophy"). They made an appearance in the [2007 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/2007_Stanley_Cup_Final "2007 Stanley Cup Final") but lost to the [Anaheim Ducks](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks") in five games.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Ottawa Senators (since 1992)](/wiki/History_of_the_Ottawa_Senators_(since_1992) "History of the Ottawa Senators (since 1992)")

[![Ottawa Senators text using decorative fonts](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/0/01/Ott_sens_campaign.png)](/wiki/File:Ott_sens_campaign.png)

Pre-launch logo used in the "Bring Back the Senators" campaign

Ottawa had been home to the [original Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators_(original) "Ottawa Senators (original)"), a founding NHL franchise and 11-time [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") champions. The original Senators' eventual financial losses forced the franchise to move to [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis") in [1934](/wiki/1934%E2%80%9335_NHL_season "1934–35 NHL season") operating as the [Eagles](/wiki/St._Louis_Eagles "St. Louis Eagles") while a Senators [senior amateur team](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators_(senior_hockey) "Ottawa Senators (senior hockey)") took over the Senators' place in Ottawa.[[7]](#cite_note-join-10) The NHL team was unsuccessful in St. Louis and planned to return to Ottawa, but the NHL decided instead to suspend the franchise and transfer the players.[[8]](#cite_note-bought-11)

Fifty-four years later, after the NHL announced plans to expand, Ottawa real estate developer [Bruce Firestone](/wiki/Bruce_Firestone "Bruce Firestone") decided along with colleagues [Cyril Leeder](/wiki/Cyril_Leeder "Cyril Leeder") and [Randy Sexton](/wiki/Randy_Sexton "Randy Sexton") that Ottawa was now able to support an NHL franchise, and the group proceeded to put a bid together. This effort was supported by key individuals including Jeff Kyle and Mark Bonneau who later became executives with the Senators.[[9]](#cite_note-12) Firestone's firm, Terrace Investments, did not have the liquid assets to finance the expansion fee and the team, but the group conceived a strategy to leverage land development. In 1989, after finding a suitable site on farmland just west of Ottawa in [Kanata](/wiki/Kanata,_Ontario "Kanata, Ontario") on which to construct a new arena, Terrace announced its intention to win a franchise and launched a successful "Bring Back the Senators" campaign to both woo the public and persuade the NHL that the city could support an NHL franchise. Public support was high, and the group would secure over 11,000 season ticket pledges.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFinnigan1992pp._196–197-13) On December 12, 1990, the NHL approved a new franchise for Firestone's group to start play in the [1992–93 season](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_NHL_season "1992–93 NHL season").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFinnigan1992201-9)

### Early years (1992–1996)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years (1992–1996)")]

The new team hired former NHL player [Mel Bridgman](/wiki/Mel_Bridgman "Mel Bridgman"), who had no previous NHL management experience, as its first [general manager](/wiki/General_manager#Sports_teams "General manager") in 1992.[[11]](#cite_note-14) Ottawa signed former [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins") head coach [Rick Bowness](/wiki/Rick_Bowness "Rick Bowness"). The new Senators were placed in the [Adams Division](/wiki/Adams_Division "Adams Division") of the [Wales Conference](/wiki/Wales_Conference "Wales Conference") and played their first game on October 8, 1992, in the [Ottawa Civic Centre](/wiki/Ottawa_Civic_Centre "Ottawa Civic Centre") against the [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens") with much pre-game spectacle.[[12]](#cite_note-auto-15) The Senators defeated the Canadiens 5–3 in one of the few highlights that season. Following the initial excitement of the opening night victory, the club floundered badly. Eventually, it tied the [San Jose Sharks](/wiki/San_Jose_Sharks "San Jose Sharks") for the worst record in the league, winning only 10 games with 70 losses and four ties for 24 points, three points better than the NHL record for futility.[[13]](#cite_note-16) The Senators had aimed low and considered the 1992–93 season a small success, as Firestone had set a goal for the season of not setting a new NHL record for fewest points in a season.[[14]](#cite_note-MacGregor1993book-17) The long-term plan was to finish low in the standings for its first few years to secure high draft picks and eventually contend for the Stanley Cup.[[14]](#cite_note-MacGregor1993book-17)

[![men on skates playing ice hockey in an arena](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Senators_civic_centre.jpg/250px-Senators_civic_centre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Senators_civic_centre.jpg)

The Senators played their home games at the [Ottawa Civic Centre](/wiki/Ottawa_Civic_Centre "Ottawa Civic Centre") from 1992 to 1996.

Terrace needed a partner to make the final franchise payment to the NHL. Firestone sold 50% of Terrace to [Rod Bryden](/wiki/Rod_Bryden "Rod Bryden"), a technology executive and entrepreneur. A limited partnership was set up to own the hockey team and a new company, Palladium Corp., which was charged with building the new arena. The partnership included local high-tech executives and singer [Paul Anka](/wiki/Paul_Anka "Paul Anka"), who was born in Ottawa.[[15]](#cite_note-18) Bryden would become the sole owner of Terrace and majority owner of the Senators in August 1993, buying out Firestone.[[16]](#cite_note-MacGregor1993-cit-19)

Bridgman was fired after one season and team president Randy Sexton took over the general manager duties. The strategy of aiming low and securing a high draft position did not change. The Senators finished last overall for the next three seasons. For the 1993–94 season, the team now played in the Eastern Conference's Northeast Division. Although 1993 first overall draft choice [Alexandre Daigle](/wiki/Alexandre_Daigle "Alexandre Daigle") wound up being one of the greatest draft busts in NHL history, they chose [Radek Bonk](/wiki/Radek_Bonk "Radek Bonk") in 1994, [Bryan Berard](/wiki/Bryan_Berard "Bryan Berard") (traded for [Wade Redden](/wiki/Wade_Redden "Wade Redden")) in 1995, [Chris Phillips](/wiki/Chris_Phillips "Chris Phillips") in 1996 and [Marian Hossa](/wiki/Mari%C3%A1n_Hossa "Marián Hossa") in 1997, all of whom would become solid NHL players and formed a strong core of players in years to come. [Alexei Yashin](/wiki/Alexei_Yashin "Alexei Yashin"), the team's first-ever draft selection from 1992, emerged as one of the NHL's brightest young stars. The team traded many of their better veteran players of the era, including 1992–93 leading scorer [Norm Maciver](/wiki/Norm_Maciver "Norm Maciver") and fan favourites [Mike Peluso](/wiki/Mike_Peluso_(ice_hockey,_born_1965) "Mike Peluso (ice hockey, born 1965)") and [Bob Kudelski](/wiki/Bob_Kudelski "Bob Kudelski") in an effort to stockpile prospects and draft picks.[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-casey-1-21)[[19]](#cite_note-casey-2-22)

As the [1995–96 season](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_NHL_season "1995–96 NHL season") began, Yashin refused to honour his contract and did not play. In December, after three straight last-place finishes and a team which was ridiculed throughout the league, fans began to grow restless waiting for the team's long-term plan to yield results, and arena attendance began to decline. Bowness was fired in late 1995 and was replaced by the [Prince Edward Island Senators](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island_Senators "Prince Edward Island Senators")' head coach [Dave Allison](/wiki/Dave_Allison "Dave Allison"). Allison would fare no better than his predecessor, and the team would stumble to a 2–22–3 record under him. Sexton himself was fired and replaced by [Pierre Gauthier](/wiki/Pierre_Gauthier "Pierre Gauthier"), the former assistant general manager of the [Mighty Ducks of Anaheim](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks") team.[[20]](#cite_note-23) Before the end of January 1996, Gauthier had resolved the team's most pressing issues by settling Yashin's contract dispute, and hiring the highly regarded [Jacques Martin](/wiki/Jacques_Martin_(ice_hockey) "Jacques Martin (ice hockey)") as head coach.[[21]](#cite_note-24) While Ottawa finished last-overall once again, the season ended with renewed optimism, due in part to the upgraded management and coaching, and also to the emergence of an unheralded rookie from Sweden named [Daniel Alfredsson](/wiki/Daniel_Alfredsson "Daniel Alfredsson"), who would win the [Calder Memorial Trophy](/wiki/Calder_Memorial_Trophy "Calder Memorial Trophy") as NHL Rookie of the Year in 1996.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarrioch1998227-25)

### Jacques Martin era (1996–2004)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Jacques Martin era (1996–2004)")]

[![tall man in hockey outfit playing hockey on ice](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Zdeno_Chara.jpg/250px-Zdeno_Chara.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zdeno_Chara.jpg)

During the [2001 NHL entry draft](/wiki/2001_NHL_entry_draft "2001 NHL entry draft"), the Senators acquired [Zdeno Chara](/wiki/Zdeno_Ch%C3%A1ra "Zdeno Chára") in a multi-player trade with the [New York Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders").

Martin would impose a "strong defence first" philosophy that led to the team qualifying for the playoffs every season that he coached, but he was criticized for the team's lack of success in the playoffs, notably losing four straight series against their provincial rival, the [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs").[[23]](#cite_note-26)

In [1996–97](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_NHL_season "1996–97 NHL season"), his first season, the club qualified for the playoffs in the last game of the season and nearly defeated the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") in the first round. In [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_NHL_season "1997–98 NHL season"), the club finished with their first winning record and upset the heavily favoured [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils") to win their first playoff series. However, they were ousted in the second round by the [Washington Capitals](/wiki/Washington_Capitals "Washington Capitals").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarrioch1998227-25) In [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_NHL_season "1998–99 NHL season"), the Senators jumped from fourteenth overall in the previous season to third, with 103 points—the first 100-point season in club history, only to be swept in the first round by the Sabres. In [1999–2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_NHL_season "1999–2000 NHL season"), despite Yashin's holdout, Martin guided the team to the playoffs, only to lose to the Maple Leafs in the first [Battle of Ontario](/wiki/Battle_of_Ontario "Battle of Ontario") series.[[24]](#cite_note-27)[[25]](#cite_note-28) Yashin returned for [2000–01](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_NHL_season "2000–01 NHL season") and the team improved to win their division and place second in the Eastern Conference. Yashin played poorly in another first-round playoff loss[[26]](#cite_note-shoalts-sweep-29) and on the day of the [2001 NHL entry draft](/wiki/2001_NHL_entry_draft "2001 NHL entry draft"), he was traded to the [New York Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders") in exchange for [Zdeno Chara](/wiki/Zdeno_Ch%C3%A1ra "Zdeno Chára"), [Bill Muckalt](/wiki/Bill_Muckalt "Bill Muckalt") and the second overall selection in the draft, which Ottawa used to select centre [Jason Spezza](/wiki/Jason_Spezza "Jason Spezza").[[27]](#cite_note-30)

The [2001–02 Senators](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Ottawa_Senators_season "2001–02 Ottawa Senators season") regular season points total dropped, but in the playoffs, they upset the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") for the franchise's second playoff series win. The Sens would go on to push their second-round series to seven games, but they were ultimately once again defeated by the Maple Leafs. Despite speculation that Martin would be fired, it was general manager [Marshall Johnston](/wiki/Marshall_Johnston "Marshall Johnston") who left, retiring from the team.[[28]](#cite_note-31) He was replaced by [John Muckler](/wiki/John_Muckler "John Muckler"), the Senators' first with previous management experience.[[29]](#cite_note-32)

Although the Senators were bankrupt, they continued to play in the [2002–03 season](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_NHL_season "2002–03 NHL season") after getting emergency financing.[[30]](#cite_note-whig-33) Despite the off-ice problems, Ottawa had an outstanding season, placing first overall in the NHL to win the [Presidents' Trophy](/wiki/Presidents%27_Trophy "Presidents' Trophy"). In the playoffs, they came within one game of making it into the [2003 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/2003_Stanley_Cup_Final "2003 Stanley Cup Final"), losing to the eventual Stanley Cup champion New Jersey Devils.[[31]](#cite_note-34) In [2003–04](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_NHL_season "2003–04 NHL season"), Martin would guide the team to another good regular season but again would lose in the first round of the playoffs to the Maple Leafs, leading to Martin's dismissal as management felt that a new coach was required for playoff success.[[32]](#cite_note-35)

#### Bankruptcy and sale to Eugene Melnyk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Bankruptcy and sale to Eugene Melnyk")]

In 2000, owner Bryden publicly appealed for tax relief from the Government of Canada for all Canadian NHL teams, coping with a significant drop in the Canadian dollar. His appeal was first met with a plan for tax relief, but the tax relief program was cancelled.[[33]](#cite_note-ct-sale-36) Bryden then announced the sale of the club outright to a limited partnership in 2002 for CA$186 million, which would include creditors and Bryden himself.[[33]](#cite_note-ct-sale-36) After its principal creditor Ogden Entertainment failed, the Senators entered bankruptcy protection in January 2003, owing CA$160 million for the club and CA$210 million for the arena.[[34]](#cite_note-37) The deal fell through in 2003 when American investor [Nelson Peltz](/wiki/Nelson_Peltz "Nelson Peltz") declined to get involved.[[35]](#cite_note-38)

In August 2003, pharmaceutical billionaire [Eugene Melnyk](/wiki/Eugene_Melnyk "Eugene Melnyk") purchased the club for a reported CA$130 million.[[36]](#cite_note-39)[[37]](#cite_note-40) Melnyk, principal shareholder of [Biovail Pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Biovail "Biovail"), chose to finance half of the purchase price for the club and arena with debt. Share values of Biovail were depressed, and he did not want to sell them at the lower price.[[38]](#cite_note-41)

### Bryan Murray era (2004–2016)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bryan Murray era (2004–2016)")]

After the playoff loss, owner Melnyk promised that changes were coming, and they came quickly. In June 2004, Anaheim Ducks general manager [Bryan Murray](/wiki/Bryan_Murray_(ice_hockey) "Bryan Murray (ice hockey)") of nearby [Shawville](/wiki/Shawville,_Quebec "Shawville, Quebec") became the head coach. That summer, the team also made substantial personnel changes, trading long-time players [Patrick Lalime](/wiki/Patrick_Lalime "Patrick Lalime")[[39]](#cite_note-42) and Radek Bonk,[[40]](#cite_note-43) and signing free agent goaltender [Dominik Hasek](/wiki/Dominik_Ha%C5%A1ek "Dominik Hašek").[[41]](#cite_note-44) The team would not be able to show its new line-up for a year, as the [2004–05 NHL lock-out](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_lock-out "2004–05 NHL lock-out") intervened and most players played in Europe or in the minors. In a final change, just before the [2005–06 season](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_NHL_season "2005–06 NHL season"), the team traded long-time player Marian Hossa for [Dany Heatley](/wiki/Dany_Heatley "Dany Heatley").[[42]](#cite_note-45)

[![man in white hockey equipment on ice skating with puck](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Daniel_Alfredsson.jpg/250px-Daniel_Alfredsson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daniel_Alfredsson.jpg)

[Daniel Alfredsson](/wiki/Daniel_Alfredsson "Daniel Alfredsson") played together with [Jason Spezza](/wiki/Jason_Spezza "Jason Spezza") and [Dany Heatley](/wiki/Dany_Heatley "Dany Heatley"), forming the CASH [line](/wiki/Line_(ice_hockey) "Line (ice hockey)"). They led the Senators to their first [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/Stanley_Cup_Final "Stanley Cup Final") appearance.

The media predicted the Senators to be Stanley Cup contenders in 2005–06, as they had a strong core of players returning. They played an up-tempo style that fit the new rule changes, and Hasek was expected to provide top-notch goaltending.[[43]](#cite_note-46) The team rushed out of the gate, winning 19 of the first 22 games, in the end winning 52 games and 113 points, placing first in the conference, and second overall. The newly formed 'CASH' line[[44]](#cite_note-47) of Alfredsson, Spezza, and Heatley established itself as one of the league's [top offensive lines](/wiki/List_of_ice_hockey_line_nicknames "List of ice hockey line nicknames").[[45]](#cite_note-48) Hasek played well until he was injured during the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"),[[46]](#cite_note-49) forcing the team to enter the playoffs with rookie netminder [Ray Emery](/wiki/Ray_Emery "Ray Emery") as their starter.[[47]](#cite_note-50) Without Hasek, the club bowed out in a second-round loss to the Buffalo Sabres.[[48]](#cite_note-51)

In [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_NHL_season "2006–07 NHL season"), the Senators reached the [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/2007_Stanley_Cup_Final "2007 Stanley Cup Final") after qualifying for the playoffs in nine consecutive seasons. The Senators had a high turn-over of personnel and the disappointment of 2006 to overcome and started the season poorly. Trade rumours swirled around Alfredsson for most of the last months of 2006. The team lifted itself out of last place in the division to nearly catch the Buffalo Sabres by season's end, placing fourth in the Eastern Conference. The team finished with 105 points, their fourth consecutive 100-point season and sixth in the last eight. In the playoffs, Ottawa continued its good play. Led by the 'CASH' line, goaltender Emery, and the strong defence of Phillips and [Anton Volchenkov](/wiki/Anton_Volchenkov "Anton Volchenkov"), the club defeated the [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins"), the second-ranked New Jersey Devils and the top-ranked Sabres to advance to the Stanley Cup Final.[[49]](#cite_note-52)

The 2006–07 Senators thus became the first Ottawa team to be in a Stanley Cup Final since [1927](/wiki/1927_Stanley_Cup_Final "1927 Stanley Cup Final"), and the city was swept up in the excitement.[[50]](#cite_note-53) Businesses along all of the main streets posted large hand-drawn "Go Sens Go" signs, residents put up large displays in front of their homes or decorated their cars.[[51]](#cite_note-54) A large Ottawa Senators flag was draped on the City Hall, along with a large video screen showing the games. A six-storey likeness of Alfredsson was hung on the Corel building.[[52]](#cite_note-55) Rallies were held outside of City Hall, car rallies of decorated cars paraded through town and a section of downtown, dubbed the "Sens Mile", was closed off to traffic during and after games for fans to congregate.[[53]](#cite_note-56)

In the Stanley Cup Final, the Senators faced the Anaheim Ducks, considered a favourite since the start of the season, a team the Senators had last played in 2006, and a team known for its strong defence. The Ducks won the first two games in Anaheim 3–2 and 1–0. Returning home, the Senators won game three 5–3 but lost game four 3–2. The Ducks won game five 6–2 in Anaheim to clinch the series and their first Stanley Cup championship. The Ducks had played outstanding defence, shutting down the 'CASH' line, forcing Murray to split up the line. The Ducks scored timely goals and Ducks' goaltender [Jean-Sebastien Giguere](/wiki/Jean-S%C3%A9bastien_Gigu%C3%A8re "Jean-Sébastien Giguère") out-played Emery.[[54]](#cite_note-cbc-finals-57)

In the off-season after the Stanley Cup Final, Murray's contract was expiring. At the same time, general manager Muckler had one season remaining and was expected to retire. Murray, who had previously been a general manager for other NHL clubs, was expected to take over the general manager position, although no public timetable was given. Owner Melnyk decided to offer Muckler another position in the organization and give the general manager position to Murray. Muckler declined the offer and was relieved from his position.[[55]](#cite_note-muckler-murray-58) Melnyk publicly justified the move, saying that he expected to lose Murray if his contract ran out.[[55]](#cite_note-muckler-murray-58) Murray then elevated [John Paddock](/wiki/John_Paddock "John Paddock"), the assistant coach, to head coach of the Senators.[[56]](#cite_note-59) Under Paddock, the team came out to a record start to the [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_NHL_season "2007–08 NHL season"). However, team play declined to a .500 level, and the team was falling out of the playoffs. Paddock was fired by Murray, who took over coaching on an interim basis.[[57]](#cite_note-60) The club managed to qualify for the playoffs by a tie-breaker but was swept in the first round of the playoffs by the Pittsburgh Penguins. In June, the club bought out goaltender Emery, who had become notorious for off-ice events in Ottawa and lateness to several team practices.[[58]](#cite_note-61)

[![man wearing black goaltender mask and hockey equipment](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Martin_Gerber.jpg/250px-Martin_Gerber.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Gerber.jpg)

[Martin Gerber](/wiki/Martin_Gerber "Martin Gerber") was a goaltender for the Senators from 2006 to 2009.

For 2008–09, Murray hired [Craig Hartsburg](/wiki/Craig_Hartsburg "Craig Hartsburg") to coach the Senators. Under Hartsburg's style, the Senators struggled and played under .500. Uneven goaltending with [Martin Gerber](/wiki/Martin_Gerber "Martin Gerber") and [Alex Auld](/wiki/Alex_Auld "Alex Auld") meant the team played cautiously to protect the goaltender. Murray's patience ran out in February 2009, with the team well out of playoff contention, and Hartsburg was fired, although he had two years left on his contract, and the team also had Paddock under contract.[[59]](#cite_note-clouston-signing-62) [Cory Clouston](/wiki/Cory_Clouston "Cory Clouston") was elevated from the [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton_Senators "Binghamton Senators") coaching position.[[59]](#cite_note-clouston-signing-62) The team played above .500 under Clouston and rookie goaltender [Brian Elliott](/wiki/Brian_Elliott "Brian Elliott"), who had been promoted from Binghamton. Gerber was waived from the team at the trading deadline, and the team traded for goaltender [Pascal Leclaire](/wiki/Pascal_Leclaire "Pascal Leclaire"), although he would not play due to injury. The team failed to make the playoffs for the first time in 12 seasons. Auld would be traded in the off-season to make room. Clouston's coaching had caused a rift with Heatley (although unspecified "personal issues" were also noted by Heatley), and after Clouston was given a contract to continue coaching, Heatley made a trade demand and was traded to the San Jose Sharks just before the start of the 2009–10 season.[[60]](#cite_note-sun-sens-trade-history-63)

In [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_NHL_season "2009–10 NHL season"), the Senators were a .500 team until going on a team-record 11-game winning streak in January. The streak propelled the team to the top of the Northeast Division standings and a top-three placing for the playoffs. The team could not hold off the Sabres for the division lead but qualified for the playoffs in the fifth position. For the third season in four, the Senators played off against the Pittsburgh Penguins in the first round. A highlight for the Senators was winning a triple-overtime fifth game in Pittsburgh,[[61]](#cite_note-64) but the team could not win a playoff game on home ice, losing the series in six games.[[62]](#cite_note-65)

The Senators had a much poorer than expected [2010–11 campaign](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_NHL_season "2010–11 NHL season"), resulting in constant rumours of a shakeup right through until December. The rumours were heightened in January after the team went on a lengthy losing streak. January was a dismal month for the Senators, winning only one game. Media speculated on the imminent firing of Clouston, Murray or both. Owner Melynk cleared the air in an article in the January 22, 2011 edition of the *[Ottawa Sun](/wiki/Ottawa_Sun "Ottawa Sun").* Melnyk stated that he would not fire either Clouston or Murray but that he had given up on this season and was in the process of developing a plan for the future.[[63]](#cite_note-66) On January 24, *[The Globe and Mail](/wiki/The_Globe_and_Mail "The Globe and Mail")* reported that the plan included hiring a new general manager before the June entry draft and that Murray would be retained as an advisor to the team. A decision on whether to retain Clouston would be made by the new general manager. The article by Roy MacGregor, a long-time reporter of the Ottawa Senators, stated that former assistant coach [Pierre McGuire](/wiki/Pierre_McGuire "Pierre McGuire") had already been interviewed.[[64]](#cite_note-67) Murray, in a press conference that day, stated that he wished to stay on as the team's general manager. He also stated that Melnyk was allowing him to continue as the general manager without restraint. Murray said that the players were now to be judged by their play until the February 28 trade deadline. Murray would attempt to move "a couple, at least," of the players for draft picks or prospects at that time if the Senators remained out of playoff contention.[[65]](#cite_note-68)

[![man wearing hockey goaltending equipment](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Craig_Anderson_2013-05-24.JPG/250px-Craig_Anderson_2013-05-24.JPG)](/wiki/File:Craig_Anderson_2013-05-24.JPG)

During the 2010–11 season, the Senators acquired [Craig Anderson](/wiki/Craig_Anderson_(ice_hockey) "Craig Anderson (ice hockey)") after swapping goaltenders with the [Colorado Avalanche](/wiki/Colorado_Avalanche "Colorado Avalanche").

True to his word, Murray made a flurry of trades. He started his overhaul with the trading of [Mike Fisher](/wiki/Mike_Fisher_(ice_hockey) "Mike Fisher (ice hockey)") to the [Nashville Predators](/wiki/Nashville_Predators "Nashville Predators"). Fisher already had a home in [Nashville](/wiki/Nashville "Nashville") with his wife [Carrie Underwood](/wiki/Carrie_Underwood "Carrie Underwood"). The trading of Fisher, a fan favourite in Ottawa, led to a small anti-Underwood backlash with the banning of her songs from the playlists of some local radio stations.[[66]](#cite_note-69) Murray next traded veterans [Chris Kelly](/wiki/Chris_Kelly_(ice_hockey) "Chris Kelly (ice hockey)"),[[67]](#cite_note-70) and [Jarkko Ruutu](/wiki/Jarkko_Ruutu "Jarkko Ruutu").[[68]](#cite_note-71) A swap of goaltenders was made with the [Colorado Avalanche](/wiki/Colorado_Avalanche "Colorado Avalanche") which brought [Craig Anderson](/wiki/Craig_Anderson_(ice_hockey) "Craig Anderson (ice hockey)") to Ottawa in exchange for Elliott, both goaltenders having sub-par years.[[69]](#cite_note-72) Next, under-achieving forward [Alex Kovalev](/wiki/Alexei_Kovalev "Alexei Kovalev") was traded to the Pittsburgh Penguins.[[70]](#cite_note-73) On trade deadline day, Ottawa picked up goaltender [Curtis McElhinney](/wiki/Curtis_McElhinney "Curtis McElhinney") on waivers. The team traded [Chris Campoli](/wiki/Chris_Campoli "Chris Campoli") with a seventh-round pick to the [Chicago Blackhawks](/wiki/Chicago_Blackhawks "Chicago Blackhawks") for a second-round pick and [Ryan Potulny](/wiki/Ryan_Potulny "Ryan Potulny").[[71]](#cite_note-74) Goaltender Anderson played very well down the stretch for Ottawa, and the team quickly signed the soon-to-be unrestricted free agent to a four-year contract.[[72]](#cite_note-75) After media speculation on the future of Murray within the organization, Murray was re-signed as general manager on April 8 to a three-year extension.[[73]](#cite_note-76) On April 9, head coach Clouston and assistants Greg Carvel and [Brad Lauer](/wiki/Brad_Lauer "Brad Lauer") were dismissed from their positions.[[74]](#cite_note-clouston-firing-77) Murray said that the decision was made based on the fact that the team entered the season believing it was a contender, but finished with a 32–40–10 record.[[74]](#cite_note-clouston-firing-77) Former [Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings")' assistant coach [Paul MacLean](/wiki/Paul_MacLean_(ice_hockey) "Paul MacLean (ice hockey)") was hired as Clouston's replacement on June 14, 2011.[[75]](#cite_note-78)

As the [2011–12 season](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_NHL_season "2011–12 NHL season") began, many hockey [writers](/wiki/Sports_journalism "Sports journalism") and [commentators](/wiki/Sports_commentator "Sports commentator") were convinced that the Senators would finish at or near the bottom of the NHL standings.[[76]](#cite_note-79) While rebuilding, the Ottawa line-up contained many [rookies](/wiki/Rookie "Rookie") and inexperienced players. The team struggled out of the gate, losing five of their first six games before a reversal of fortunes saw them win six games in a row. In December 2011, the team acquired forward [Kyle Turris](/wiki/Kyle_Turris "Kyle Turris") from the [Phoenix Coyotes](/wiki/Phoenix_Coyotes "Phoenix Coyotes") in exchange for highly regarded prospect [David Rundblad](/wiki/David_Rundblad "David Rundblad") and a draft pick.[[77]](#cite_note-80) The team improved its play afterwards and moved into a playoff position before the All-Star Game. For the first time in Senators' history, the All-Star Game was held in Ottawa and considered a great success. Five Senators were voted in or named to the event, including Daniel Alfredsson, who was named the captain of one team.[[78]](#cite_note-81) The team continued its playoff push after the break. After starting goaltender Anderson injured his hand in a [kitchen](/wiki/Kitchen "Kitchen") accident at home, the Senators called up [Robin Lehner](/wiki/Robin_Lehner "Robin Lehner") from Binghamton and acquired highly regarded goaltender [Ben Bishop](/wiki/Ben_Bishop "Ben Bishop") from the [St. Louis Blues](/wiki/St._Louis_Blues "St. Louis Blues").[[79]](#cite_note-82) While Anderson recovered, the team continued its solid play and finished as the eighth seed in the Eastern Conference, drawing a first-round playoff matchup against the Conference champion [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers"). Ultimately, Ottawa lost the series in seven games.[[80]](#cite_note-83)

[![man with moustache in suit](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Paul_MacLean_2013-05-24.JPG/250px-Paul_MacLean_2013-05-24.JPG)](/wiki/File:Paul_MacLean_2013-05-24.JPG)

[Paul MacLean](/wiki/Paul_MacLean_(ice_hockey) "Paul MacLean (ice hockey)") was awarded the [Jack Adams Award](/wiki/Jack_Adams_Award "Jack Adams Award") during the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_NHL_season "2012–13 NHL season"). He was the Senators' head coach from 2011 to 2014.

The [next season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_NHL_season "2012–13 NHL season"), Ottawa would be challenged to repeat the success they had in 2011–12 due to long-term injuries to key players such as [Erik Karlsson](/wiki/Erik_Karlsson "Erik Karlsson"), Jason Spezza, [Milan Michalek](/wiki/Milan_Mich%C3%A1lek "Milan Michálek") and Craig Anderson.[[81]](#cite_note-adams-win-84) Despite these injuries, the Senators would finish seventh in the Eastern Conference and head coach Paul MacLean would go on to win the Jack Adams Award as the NHL's coach of the year.[[81]](#cite_note-adams-win-84) In a rivalry series, Ottawa defeated the second-seeded Montreal Canadiens in the first round of the playoffs in five games, blowing out Montreal 6–1 in games three and five.[[82]](#cite_note-85) This was the first Montreal-Ottawa playoff series since Ottawa joined the league and the first between the cities' teams since the original Senators played the Canadiens in 1927.[[83]](#cite_note-86) The Senators could not repeat the upset, losing to the top-seeded Pittsburgh Penguins in five games in the second round.[[84]](#cite_note-87)

July 5, 2013, would be a day of mixed emotions for the city and fans, as long-time captain Alfredsson signed a one-year contract with the Detroit Red Wings, leaving Ottawa after 17 seasons with the Senators and 14 as captain after a contract dispute.[[85]](#cite_note-panzeri-2013-88) The signing shocked numerous fans across the city and many within the Senators organization.[[85]](#cite_note-panzeri-2013-88) The day finished optimistically, however, as Murray acquired star forward [Bobby Ryan](/wiki/Bobby_Ryan "Bobby Ryan") from the Anaheim Ducks, hoping Ryan could replace Alfredsson on the top line with Spezza. Murray would also sign free-agent forward [Clarke MacArthur](/wiki/Clarke_MacArthur "Clarke MacArthur") to a two-year contract that same day and bring back former defenceman [Joe Corvo](/wiki/Joe_Corvo "Joe Corvo") to a one-year contract three days later on July 8, 2013.[[86]](#cite_note-89)

For the [2013–14 season](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_NHL_season "2013–14 NHL season"), the league realigned and Ottawa was assigned to the new Atlantic Division along with the rest of the old Northeast Division and the Detroit Red Wings, formerly of the [Western Conference](/wiki/Western_Conference_(NHL) "Western Conference (NHL)").[[87]](#cite_note-90) The re-alignment brought increased competition to qualify for the playoffs, as there were now 16 teams in the Eastern Conference fighting for eight playoff spots. The season began with a changing of leadership, as on September 14, 2013, the Ottawa Senators named Spezza their eighth captain in franchise history.[[88]](#cite_note-91) While new addition Clarke MacArthur had a career year, Ryan and Spezza struggled to find chemistry, and Ryan was moved to a line with MacArthur and Kyle Turris.[[89]](#cite_note-92) Corvo lost his place in the line-up and was waived.[[90]](#cite_note-93) The team outside of a playoff position, Murray bolstered the club with a trade for flashy right-winger [Ales Hemsky](/wiki/Ale%C5%A1_Hemsk%C3%BD "Aleš Hemský") from the [Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers").[[91]](#cite_note-94) The club, however, was eliminated from playoff contention in the last week of the season, finishing five points short.[[92]](#cite_note-95) Further disappointment ensued as the team lost Hemsky to free agency and Spezza requested a trade out of Ottawa, ending the era of the stars of the 2007 Stanley Cup Final team.[[93]](#cite_note-96) Spezza agreed to be traded to the Dallas Stars and was sent with [Ludwig Karlsson](/wiki/Ludwig_Karlsson "Ludwig Karlsson") for [Alex Chiasson](/wiki/Alex_Chiasson "Alex Chiasson"), [Nick Paul](/wiki/Nick_Paul "Nick Paul"), Alex Guptill and a [2015](/wiki/2015_NHL_entry_draft "2015 NHL entry draft") second-round pick.[[94]](#cite_note-97)

[![man with mustache and beard wearing a white ice hockey uniform](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Erik_Karlsson_1_2017-05-13.jpg/250px-Erik_Karlsson_1_2017-05-13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Erik_Karlsson_1_2017-05-13.jpg)

[Erik Karlsson](/wiki/Erik_Karlsson "Erik Karlsson") was team captain through the [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_NHL_season "2014–15 NHL season") to [2017–18](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_NHL_season "2017–18 NHL season") seasons.

At the beginning of the [2014–15 season](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_NHL_season "2014–15 NHL season"), Karlsson was named the franchise's ninth captain and the club signed Ryan to a seven-year extension.[[95]](#cite_note-98) Unhappy with an 11–11–5 record after 27 games, the Senators fired head coach MacLean and replaced him with assistant coach [Dave Cameron](/wiki/Dave_Cameron_(ice_hockey) "Dave Cameron (ice hockey)").[[96]](#cite_note-99) The change turned the season around for the Senators, who won 32 of their last 55 games. After both Senators' goalies Anderson and Lehner were injured, the team turned to Binghamton goaltender [Andrew Hammond](/wiki/Andrew_Hammond_(ice_hockey) "Andrew Hammond (ice hockey)"). Hammond, aka 'The Hamburglar,' would compile a record of 20–1–2, a [goals-against average](/wiki/Goals-against_average "Goals-against average") of 1.79, and a [save percentage](/wiki/Save_percentage "Save percentage") of .941 to get the team back into playoff position.[[97]](#cite_note-100)[[98]](#cite_note-101) The Senators became the first team in modern NHL history to overcome a 14-point deficit at any juncture of the season to qualify for the playoffs.[[99]](#cite_note-102) However, the Senators lost to the Canadiens in six games in the first round of the playoffs.[[100]](#cite_note-103)

During the 2014–15 season, it was announced that Murray had cancer. Taking regular treatment, Murray chose to stay on as general manager through the [2015–16 season](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_NHL_season "2015–16 NHL season"). Despite posting the best record of any Canadian team in the league, the Senators failed to make the playoffs in what was considered a disappointing season (all seven Canadian teams missed the playoffs). Murray made one 'blockbuster' nine-player trade that brought Toronto Maple Leafs' captain [Dion Phaneuf](/wiki/Dion_Phaneuf "Dion Phaneuf") to the Senators before the trade deadline.[[101]](#cite_note-104) The Senators were outside of a playoff position at the time of the deal, and played well until the end of the season, but fell just short, placing fifth in the division.[[102]](#cite_note-105)

### Pierre Dorion era (2016–2023)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Pierre Dorion era (2016–2023)")]

On April 10, 2016, the day after the final game of the 2015–16 season, Murray announced his resignation as general manager and that he would continue in an advisory role with the club. Assistant general manager [Pierre Dorion](/wiki/Pierre_Dorion "Pierre Dorion") was promoted to the general manager position.[[103]](#cite_note-106) On April 12, the Senators fired head coach Cameron.[[104]](#cite_note-107) On May 8, 2016, the Senators hired former [Tampa Bay Lightning](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Lightning "Tampa Bay Lightning") head coach [Guy Boucher](/wiki/Guy_Boucher "Guy Boucher") as their new head coach.[[105]](#cite_note-108) On the following day, [Marc Crawford](/wiki/Marc_Crawford "Marc Crawford") was announced as associate coach.[[106]](#cite_note-109) On June 13, 2016, the Senators hired Daniel Alfredsson as the senior advisor of hockey operations.[[107]](#cite_note-110) In June 2016, the Senators hired [Rob Cookson](/wiki/Rob_Cookson "Rob Cookson") as an assistant coach, who had worked with both Boucher and Crawford in Switzerland, and Pierre Groulx as a goaltending coach.[[108]](#cite_note-111)

The Senators finished second in the Atlantic Division during the [2016–17 season](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_NHL_season "2016–17 NHL season"). They faced the Boston Bruins in the first round of the playoffs, winning that series in six games. In the second round, they defeated the New York Rangers in six games. During the second game of that series, [Jean-Gabriel Pageau](/wiki/Jean-Gabriel_Pageau "Jean-Gabriel Pageau") scored four goals, including the game-winning goal in double overtime. The Senators would come within one win of the [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/2017_Stanley_Cup_Final "2017 Stanley Cup Final") having lost in double overtime of the seventh game of their conference finals series against the Pittsburgh Penguins, who went on to win their second consecutive Stanley Cup.[[109]](#cite_note-112)[[110]](#cite_note-113)

Following their appearance in the conference finals the previous season, the Senators lost defenceman [Marc Methot](/wiki/Marc_Methot "Marc Methot") to the [2017 NHL expansion draft](/wiki/2017_NHL_expansion_draft "2017 NHL expansion draft"). On November 5, 2017, the Senators conducted a blockbuster trade with the Colorado Avalanche, bringing in star forward [Matt Duchene](/wiki/Matt_Duchene "Matt Duchene") from the Avalanche in exchange for Kyle Turris, [Shane Bowers](/wiki/Shane_Bowers_(ice_hockey) "Shane Bowers (ice hockey)"), Andrew Hammond, a conditional first-round pick in [2018](/wiki/2018_NHL_entry_draft "2018 NHL entry draft") or [2019](/wiki/2019_NHL_entry_draft "2019 NHL entry draft") and a third-round pick in 2019. Following the trade, however, the Senators' season began to fall apart with a disastrous November road trip.[[111]](#cite_note-heritage-classic-114) A season highlight was hosting the [NHL 100 Classic](/wiki/NHL_100_Classic "NHL 100 Classic") game outdoors at the [TD Place Stadium](/wiki/TD_Place_Stadium "TD Place Stadium") football field versus the Montreal Canadiens. The game marked the centennial of the first Montreal-Ottawa game in the NHL. The Senators won the game 3–0, but the festival atmosphere was somewhat marred by owner Melnyk's controversial comments to the press about attendance levels and selling or moving the team.[[111]](#cite_note-heritage-classic-114) Out of the playoff picture, the Senators chose to trade away veteran players. Forward [Derick Brassard](/wiki/Derick_Brassard "Derick Brassard") and defenceman Phaneuf were dealt at the trade deadline to the Pittsburgh Penguins and [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings"), respectively. The Senators finished the year second-to-last in the league with a 28–43–11 record and 67 points, their fourth-worst season since entering the league.[[112]](#cite_note-115)[[113]](#cite_note-116)

During the 2018 off-season, the Senators began what would end up being a complete rebuild. They traded forward [Mike Hoffman](/wiki/Mike_Hoffman_(ice_hockey,_born_1989) "Mike Hoffman (ice hockey, born 1989)") to the San Jose Sharks, who later that day flipped him to the [Florida Panthers](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers"). The Senators ended up with the fourth-overall pick in the 2018 draft due to their poor record. Under the Matt Duchene trade conditions, they either had to give up the pick to the Avalanche or wait a year and surrender their 2019 first-round pick instead. The Senators elected to keep the pick and selected forward [Brady Tkachuk](/wiki/Brady_Tkachuk "Brady Tkachuk") fourth overall. Just before the regular season started, the Senators traded their captain, Erik Karlsson, to the San Jose Sharks for a large package of players and draft picks.[[114]](#cite_note-117)[[115]](#cite_note-118)

After a miserable start to the [2018–19 season](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_NHL_season "2018–19 NHL season"), the Senators were unable to re-sign forwards Duchene, [Mark Stone](/wiki/Mark_Stone "Mark Stone"), and [Ryan Dzingel](/wiki/Ryan_Dzingel "Ryan Dzingel") before the trade deadline. In an attempt to create optimism, owner Melnyk famously stated: "The Senators will be all-in again for a five-year run of unparalleled success–where the team will plan to spend close to the NHL's salary cap every year from 2021 to 2025. The Senators' current rebuild is a blueprint on how to bring the Stanley Cup home to its rightful place in Ottawa."[[116]](#cite_note-119) All three players were subsequently traded before the 2019 trade deadline. Duchene and Dzingel were traded to the Columbus Blue Jackets in exchange for draft picks, prospects and [Anthony Duclair](/wiki/Anthony_Duclair "Anthony Duclair"). In contrast, fan favourite Stone was traded to the [Vegas Golden Knights](/wiki/Vegas_Golden_Knights "Vegas Golden Knights") in exchange for prospect [Erik Brannstrom](/wiki/Erik_Br%C3%A4nnstr%C3%B6m "Erik Brännström"), forward [Oscar Lindberg](/wiki/Oscar_Lindberg_(ice_hockey) "Oscar Lindberg (ice hockey)") and a second-round pick.[[117]](#cite_note-120) Just days after trading away the team's three leading scorers, it was announced that the plans for a new downtown arena on the open land at [LeBreton Flats](/wiki/LeBreton_Flats "LeBreton Flats") had fallen through. The *[Ottawa Citizen](/wiki/Ottawa_Citizen "Ottawa Citizen")* called it "one of the gloomiest weeks in the history of the Ottawa Senators."[[118]](#cite_note-121) On March 1, 2019, with the team in 31st place, head coach Boucher was fired with associate coach Marc Crawford taking over as head coach for the remainder of the season.[[119]](#cite_note-122) The 2018–19 season saw the team finish last in the NHL without their own first-round draft pick. This marked the first time since 1995–96 that the Senators missed back-to-back playoff appearances.[[120]](#cite_note-123)

Prior to the [2019–20 season](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_NHL_season "2019–20 NHL season"), [D. J. Smith](/wiki/D._J._Smith_(ice_hockey) "D. J. Smith (ice hockey)") was hired as the new head coach while the organization shifted its focus to developing its young players. The season was ultimately cut short due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_North_America "COVID-19 pandemic in North America"), and the Senators finished second last in the NHL with 62 points in 71 games. In contrast, Ottawa's farm team, the [Belleville Senators](/wiki/Belleville_Senators "Belleville Senators"), put together a very impressive, albeit shortened season led by Ottawa's top prospects, which included [Josh Norris](/wiki/Josh_Norris "Josh Norris"), [Drake Batherson](/wiki/Drake_Batherson "Drake Batherson"), [Alex Formenton](/wiki/Alex_Formenton "Alex Formenton") and Erik Brannstrom among others.[[121]](#cite_note-124) Meanwhile, the San Jose Sharks suffered an unexpected collapse that year which significantly benefited the Senators who had acquired their first-round draft pick in the Karlsson trade.[[122]](#cite_note-125) Ottawa found themselves with the third and fifth picks in the [2020 NHL draft](/wiki/2020_NHL_entry_draft "2020 NHL entry draft") and used them to select highly touted prospects [Tim Stutzle](/wiki/Tim_St%C3%BCtzle "Tim Stützle") and [Jake Sanderson](/wiki/Jake_Sanderson "Jake Sanderson").[[123]](#cite_note-126)

The Senators would miss the playoffs again for the [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_NHL_season "2020–21 NHL season"), a season overshadowed by the COVID-19 pandemic. The young team played an all-Canadian shortened season, during which they had a poor record to start the season but finished the season with a strong stretch of play, inspiring some optimism for the future.[[124]](#cite_note-127) The Senators again traded away veterans at the trade deadline for draft picks.[[125]](#cite_note-128)

Before the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Ottawa_Senators_season "2021–22 Ottawa Senators season"), general manager Dorion's contract was extended until 2025. He proceeded to declare: "The rebuild is done. Now we're stepping into another zone." His claims, however, did not materialize as the Senators got off to a slow start and were quickly out of the playoff picture.[[126]](#cite_note-129) On October 17, 2021, Tkachuk signed a seven-year deal after a dramatic contract holdout.[[127]](#cite_note-130) Just under three weeks later, he was named the tenth captain in franchise history at just 22 years of age. Tkachuk was, at the time, the franchise's youngest-ever captain.[[128]](#cite_note-131)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Ottawa_Senators_at_Seattle_Kraken_-_2023-03-09_-_Brady_Tkachuk.jpg/250px-Ottawa_Senators_at_Seattle_Kraken_-_2023-03-09_-_Brady_Tkachuk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ottawa_Senators_at_Seattle_Kraken_-_2023-03-09_-_Brady_Tkachuk.jpg)

Current captain Brady Tkachuk

Ahead of the [2022–23 season](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_NHL_season "2022–23 NHL season"), the team was aggressive in their efforts to exit their rebuild, drastically retooling the team through the acquisitions of forwards [Alex DeBrincat](/wiki/Alex_DeBrincat "Alex DeBrincat") and [Claude Giroux](/wiki/Claude_Giroux "Claude Giroux") and goaltender [Cam Talbot](/wiki/Cam_Talbot "Cam Talbot").[[129]](#cite_note-132)[[130]](#cite_note-133)[[131]](#cite_note-134) In addition, the team signed forwards Norris and Stutzle to eight-year contract extensions.[[132]](#cite_note-135) At the end of the season, the Senators missed the playoffs by six points.[[133]](#cite_note-136)

Before the [2023–24 season](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_NHL_season "2023–24 NHL season"), DeBrincat notified the Senators he would not re-sign long-term with the club and was traded to Detroit. Talbot was not re-signed. Instead, the Senators signed free agents – goaltender [Joonas Korpisalo](/wiki/Joonas_Korpisalo "Joonas Korpisalo") and forward [Vladimir Tarasenko](/wiki/Vladimir_Tarasenko "Vladimir Tarasenko"). At the start of the 2023–24 season, the NHL levied the forfeiture of a first-round pick due to negligence on the part of the Senators involving the trade of [Evgeni Dadonov](/wiki/Evgeni_Dadonov "Evgeni Dadonov"). Dorion resigned as general manager upon being asked to step down.[[134]](#cite_note-forfeit-137) After an 11–15–0 start to the season, the Senators fired coach Smith on December 18, 2023 and former head coach Martin, who had been serving as a senior advisor stepped in as interim coach until the end of the season.[[135]](#cite_note-138)

### Death of owner Eugene Melnyk and sale

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Death of owner Eugene Melnyk and sale")]

Owner Eugene Melnyk died in March 2022 due to an unspecified illness.[[136]](#cite_note-139) In statements in recent years, Melnyk had said that he planned to leave the team to his two daughters Olivia and Anna when he had been asked if he intended to sell the team. However, there had been speculation about ownership changes.[[137]](#cite_note-140) The team added an 'EM' patch on the jersey for the rest of the season.[[138]](#cite_note-141) In November 2022, the team engaged a New York City investment banker to facilitate a sale of the team.[[139]](#cite_note-142) The Senators confirmed the planned sale in a press release on November 5, with a condition of sale being that the team remain in Ottawa.[[140]](#cite_note-143) On June 13, 2023, the Senators announced that a purchase agreement had been signed with a group of investors headed by [Michael Andlauer](/wiki/Michael_Andlauer "Michael Andlauer"), a Toronto businessman and part-owner of the Montreal Canadiens.[[141]](#cite_note-144) Andlauer became majority owner along with his partners, Eugene Melnyk's daughters, and a group of Canadian businessmen on September 21, 2023.[[142]](#cite_note-Michael_Andlauer_is_the_new_owner_of_the_Ottawa_Senators-145)

### Andlauer era and 2024–25 playoff berth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Andlauer era and 2024–25 playoff berth")]

After Andlauer took over the franchise, he set about making changes. Cyril Leeder, who had previously served as the team's chief executive officer (CEO) from 2009 to 2017 was brought back as the CEO in September 2023.[[143]](#cite_note-146) On September 29, former player [Steve Staios](/wiki/Steve_Staios "Steve Staios") was named president of hockey operations.[[144]](#cite_note-147) After Dorion's failure to disclose contract information led to the NHL penalizing the team a first-round draft pick, he resigned. Dorion was replaced as general manager on an interim basis by Staios.[[134]](#cite_note-forfeit-137) Staios was later named permanent general manager and another former NHL player, [Dave Poulin](/wiki/Dave_Poulin "Dave Poulin"), was named senior vice president of hockey operations to assist Staios in December.[[145]](#cite_note-148)

During the off-season after the 2023–24 season, the Senators announced the hiring of former Canucks and Devils head coach [Travis Green](/wiki/Travis_Green "Travis Green").[[146]](#cite_note-149) Green's first season behind the Senators' bench got off to a shaky start, and by the middle of the [2024–25 season](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_NHL_season "2024–25 NHL season") the team was 28th in the league. The team improved its position as the season progressed and clinched its first playoff berth to end an eight-year drought. Green became the first head coach to lead the Senators to the postseason since the 2016–17 season.[[147]](#cite_note-150) In the playoffs, the Senators faced off against the Toronto Maple Leafs in the first postseason installment of the Battle of Ontario since the [2004 playoffs](/wiki/2004_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2004 Stanley Cup playoffs"). The Maple Leafs began the series with a 3–0 series lead, but the Senators would win the next two games before being eliminated in six games.[[148]](#cite_note-151)

On August 27, 2025, it was announced that the [Allen Americans](/wiki/Allen_Americans "Allen Americans") of the [ECHL](/wiki/ECHL "ECHL") will once again be an official minor-league affiliate of the team, as well as the Belleville Senators of the AHL starting with the [2025–26 season](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_ECHL_season "2025–26 ECHL season").[[149]](#cite_note-152)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

### Toronto Maple Leafs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Toronto Maple Leafs")]

Main article: [Battle of Ontario](/wiki/Battle_of_Ontario "Battle of Ontario")

The Battle of Ontario is a rivalry with the Toronto Maple Leafs.[[150]](#cite_note-153)[[151]](#cite_note-154) It is the current version of an old rivalry between Ottawa, Canada's capital, and [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), Canada's largest city and business capital. The teams compete in the [same division](/wiki/Atlantic_Division_(NHL) "Atlantic Division (NHL)") and meet frequently during regular season games. In the early 2000s, the teams met four consecutive times in the playoffs with Toronto winning all four series.

As of the end of the 2024–25 season, Ottawa leads the regular season series 76–55–3–13, while Toronto leads 20–10 in the playoff record.[[152]](#cite_note-WLrecords-155)

### Montreal Canadiens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Montreal Canadiens")]

There is a long-standing Ottawa-Montreal rivalry (dating back to the 1800s) in multiple sports including ice hockey.[[153]](#cite_note-156)[[154]](#cite_note-157) Today, both teams compete in the Atlantic Division. There is only a two-hour drive from [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") to Ottawa via [Quebec Autoroute 40](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_40 "Quebec Autoroute 40") and [Ontario Highway 417](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_417 "Ontario Highway 417"), plus railway and air connections. When the teams play in Ottawa, "the games matter" according to sports writer Wayne Scanlan of the *[Ottawa Citizen](/wiki/Ottawa_Citizen "Ottawa Citizen")*, and many Canadiens fans turn out in Montreal jerseys.[[155]](#cite_note-Scanlan-158) The current rivalry began when the Ottawa Senators' first NHL game was held in Ottawa on October 8, 1992, where the expansion Senators beat the Canadiens 5–3.[[155]](#cite_note-Scanlan-158) That victory was one of the only Senators' highlights of their inaugural season; they won only nine more games the rest of the season to finish with 10 wins and 24 points, while the Canadiens won their [24th Stanley Cup](/wiki/1993_Stanley_Cup_Final "1993 Stanley Cup Final") that season. Another regular season highlight of the rivalry was the NHL 100 Classic played at TD Place Stadium outdoors in Ottawa in December 2017, celebrating the centennial of the first NHL game between the original Senators and the Canadiens.

The rivalry existed prior to the teams meeting in the playoffs, especially as the teams both became competitive in the early 2000s and Ottawa's captain Alfredsson was among those who hoped for a playoff series between the two.[[156]](#cite_note-159)[[157]](#cite_note-160) The hoped-for playoff series finally came in [2013](/wiki/2013_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2013 Stanley Cup playoffs"). In that series, there were a large number of controversial events. In game one, Ottawa's [Eric Gryba](/wiki/Eric_Gryba "Eric Gryba") laid out Montreal's [Lars Eller](/wiki/Lars_Eller "Lars Eller") in an open ice hit. After the game, the Senators' head coach MacLean blamed [Raphael Diaz](/wiki/Raphael_Diaz "Raphael Diaz") for a suicide pass.[[158]](#cite_note-161) Later, Canadiens' coach [Michel Therrien](/wiki/Michel_Therrien "Michel Therrien") responded and said that what MacLean said was a "lack of respect." Ottawa won that game 4–2. [Brandon Prust](/wiki/Brandon_Prust "Brandon Prust") later insulted MacLean after the game, saying that he doesn't care what that "bug-eyed, fat walrus has to say."[[159]](#cite_note-162) In game three, there was a full line brawl between Ottawa and Montreal. And later in that game, MacLean called a timeout with 17 seconds left in the third period with a 6–1 lead. Therrien called Maclean classless while Maclean responded by saying that he was protecting his players from Montreal's dirty play in that game.[[160]](#cite_note-163) The Senators eventually won the series 4–1.

[Two years later](/wiki/2015_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2015 Stanley Cup playoffs"), the rivalry was renewed in another playoff series.[[161]](#cite_note-164) In game one, Montreal's [P. K. Subban](/wiki/P._K._Subban "P. K. Subban") slashed Ottawa's Stone – breaking his wrist – and Subban was later ejected.[[162]](#cite_note-165) Senators' coach [Dave Cameron](/wiki/Dave_Cameron_(ice_hockey) "Dave Cameron (ice hockey)") called the slash vicious and said that Subban deserved a suspension.[[163]](#cite_note-166) Ottawa's Clarke MacArthur called it a 'lumberjack slash', and Stone said that he was being targeted all game. Meanwhile, Therrien said that Subban did not deserve to be ejected and should have only gotten a minor penalty. The Canadiens won that game 4–3. The Canadiens won the next two games in overtime. Riding a 3–0 lead in the series, the Canadiens saw Ottawa win the next two games, before closing the series in game six with a 2–0 victory in Ottawa.

As of the end of the 2024–25 season, Montreal leads the current Ottawa Senators in the regular season series 82–73–5–11, while Ottawa leads 6–5 in the playoff record.[[152]](#cite_note-WLrecords-155)

## Home rinks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Home rinks")]

### Ottawa Civic Centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ottawa Civic Centre")]

Further information: [TD Place Arena](/wiki/TD_Place_Arena "TD Place Arena")

The Senators' first home arena was the Ottawa Civic Centre (now TD Place Arena), located on Bank Street in Ottawa, where they played from the 1992–93 season to January of the 1995–96 season. The arena, used by the junior [Ottawa 67's](/wiki/Ottawa_67%27s "Ottawa 67's"), was renovated for the Senators, including adding press boxes and luxury boxes. They played their first home game on October 8, 1992, against the Montreal Canadiens with much pre-game spectacle. The Senators would defeat the Canadiens 5–3.[[12]](#cite_note-auto-15) Their last game in the arena was on December 31, 1995, versus the Tampa Bay Lightning.[[164]](#cite_note-167)[[165]](#cite_note-168)

### Canadian Tire Centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Canadian Tire Centre")]

Further information: [Canadian Tire Centre](/wiki/Canadian_Tire_Centre "Canadian Tire Centre")

As part of its bid to land an NHL franchise for Ottawa, Terrace Corporation unveiled the original proposal for the arena development at a press conference in September 1989. The proposal included a hotel and 20,500-seat arena, named The Palladium, on 100 acres (0.40 km2), surrounded by a 500-acre (2.0 km2) mini-city, named "West Terrace." The site itself, 600 acres (2.4 km2) of farmland, on the western border of Kanata, had been acquired in May 1987 from farmer Cyril Bennett for CA$1 million,[[166]](#cite_note-169) and flipped to Terrace for CA$2.6 million in 1989.[[167]](#cite_note-170) Rezoning approval was granted by the [Ontario Municipal Board](/wiki/Ontario_Municipal_Board "Ontario Municipal Board") on August 28, 1991, with conditions.[[168]](#cite_note-oc-omb-171) The conditions imposed by the board included scaling down the arena to 18,500 seats, a moratorium on development outside the initial 100-acre (0.40 km2) arena site, and that the cost of the highway interchange with Highway 417 be paid by Terrace.[[168]](#cite_note-oc-omb-171) A two-year period was spent seeking financing for the site and interchange by Terrace Corporation. The corporation received a CA$6 million grant from the [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") but needed to borrow to pay for the rest of the costs of construction. A ground-breaking ceremony was held in June 1992, but construction did not start until July 7, 1994. Actual construction took 18 months, finishing in January 1996.[[169]](#cite_note-172)

[![People milling about a large brick color building with large sign Canadian Tire Centre](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Canadian_Tire_Centre_exterior_before_a_match_in_2022.jpg/250px-Canadian_Tire_Centre_exterior_before_a_match_in_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canadian_Tire_Centre_exterior_before_a_match_in_2022.jpg)

The Senators moved to [Canadian Tire Centre](/wiki/Canadian_Tire_Centre "Canadian Tire Centre") in 1996. The arena is their second and current home arena.

The Palladium opened on January 15, 1996, with a concert by Canadian rocker [Bryan Adams](/wiki/Bryan_Adams "Bryan Adams").[[170]](#cite_note-173) The Senators played their first game in their new arena two days later, falling 3–0 to the Montreal Canadiens. On February 17, 1996, the name 'Palladium' was changed to 'Corel Centre' when [Corel Corporation](/wiki/Corel_Corporation "Corel Corporation"), an Ottawa software company, signed a twenty-year deal for the naming rights.[[171]](#cite_note-174)[[172]](#cite_note-175)

When mortgage holder [Covanta Energy](/wiki/Covanta_Energy "Covanta Energy") (the former Ogden Entertainment) went into receivership in 2001, Terrace was expected to pay off its debt to Covanta in full. The ownership could not refinance the arena, eventually leading Terrace itself to declare bankruptcy in 2002.[[30]](#cite_note-whig-33) On August 26, 2003, billionaire businessman Eugene Melnyk finalized the purchase of the Senators and the arena.[[173]](#cite_note-sbank_site-176) The arena and club became solely owned by Melnyk through a new company, Capital Sports & Entertainment.[[174]](#cite_note-melnyk-death-announce-177)

In 2004, the ownership applied to expand its seating, and the City of Ottawa amended its by-laws for the venue, increasing its [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") in 2005 to 19,153 and total attendance capacity to 20,500, including standing room.[[173]](#cite_note-sbank_site-176)[[175]](#cite_note-178)

On January 19, 2006, the arena became known as 'Scotiabank Place' after Melnyk reached a new 15-year naming agreement with Canadian bank [Scotiabank](/wiki/Scotiabank "Scotiabank") on January 11, 2006, ending the 20-year contract with Corel.[[176]](#cite_note-179)[[177]](#cite_note-180) Scotiabank had been an advertising partner with the club for several years and a financial partner with owner Melnyk, and signed a CA$20 million over 15-years deal; a slight increase over Corel's contract.[[178]](#cite_note-sp-name-change-181) While Corel was no longer the arena name sponsor, it continued as an advertising sponsor.[[178]](#cite_note-sp-name-change-181)

In 2011, in time for the Senators hosting the NHL All-Star Game, the team installed a new video scoreboard, known as the 'Bell HD' screen, made by Panasonic.[[179]](#cite_note-new-screen-182) The new scoreboard increased the video display from 700 square feet (65 m2) to 2,170 square feet (202 m2) and added LED rings.[[179]](#cite_note-new-screen-182)

On June 18, 2013, the Senators and Scotiabank ended the seven-year naming rights deal. The Ottawa Senators announced a marketing agreement with the [Canadian Tire](/wiki/Canadian_Tire "Canadian Tire") retail store chain, and as a result, the arena was renamed Canadian Tire Centre on July 1, 2013.[[180]](#cite_note-183)

### Downtown arena proposal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Downtown arena proposal")]

Main article: [New Ottawa Arena](/wiki/New_Ottawa_Arena "New Ottawa Arena")

In 2015, the [National Capital Commission](/wiki/National_Capital_Commission "National Capital Commission") (NCC) put out a request for proposals to redevelop the LeBreton Flats area in downtown Ottawa, a longtime vacant former industrial area. In 2016, the NCC settled on the proposal presented by Senators owner Eugene Melnyk and the RendezVous LeBreton Group partnership with Trinity Developments.[[181]](#cite_note-RendezVous-184) The proposal included housing units, park space, a recreation facility, a library and a new arena for the Ottawa Senators.[[182]](#cite_note-185)

The plan to build a new arena downtown came apart in late 2018 after it was revealed that the Senators were suing Trinity for CA$700 million in damages. Trinity was developing a site adjacent to the LeBreton Flats site, and the Senators felt this was inappropriate competition. Trinity responded with a CA$1 billion lawsuit, accusing the Senators of being unwilling to contribute any money to the project. The NCC announced the cancellation of the partnership's bid to develop the site but gave the sides an extension when the two parties agreed to mediation. On February 27, 2019, it was announced that mediation between the parties had failed to come to an agreement and that the NCC would explore other options for the site's redevelopment.[[183]](#cite_note-186)[[184]](#cite_note-187)

The NCC resumed the process to redevelop the overall site, reserving the site of the arena and asking for preliminary bids on the arena site separately. After a February 2022 deadline to submit bids, the NCC announced that it had received several bids for the site.[[185]](#cite_note-188) Local media speculated that the Senators were actively pursuing a bid, authorized by Melnyk shortly before his death.[[186]](#cite_note-189) On June 23, 2022, the NCC announced that the Senators proposal had been chosen for the site, with a lease agreement expected to be put in place by autumn of 2023.[[187]](#cite_note-190) In related business, the outstanding lawsuits around the previous LeBreton bid were settled out of court in December 2022.[[188]](#cite_note-191)

In 2024, the Senators stated that it would be "several years" before construction would start. The Senators under Andlauer reviewed other possibilities before concluding that they would like to instead purchase the site, and possibly expand the site they could develop as an entertainment destination. In September 2024, the Senators and the NCC agreed to expand the site to 10 acres (4.0 ha) from 6 acres, but the purchase has not been concluded. A land sale agreement, for LeBreton Flats, was signed in August 2025 with the NCC.[[189]](#cite_note-192)

### Practice facility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Practice facility")]

The Senators practice facility is known as the [Bell Sensplex](/wiki/Bell_Sensplex "Bell Sensplex"), a CA$25.6 million joint venture with the City of Ottawa. Located southeast of the Canadian Tire Centre, the facility has three NHL-sized rinks, an Olympics-size rink and a fieldhouse that opened in 2004.[[190]](#cite_note-193) It is used for Senators' practices, minor hockey and it is also the home of the annual minor hockey league [Bell Capital Cup](/wiki/Bell_Capital_Cup "Bell Capital Cup") tournament.[[191]](#cite_note-194)

## Team identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Team identity")]

The Senators organization is located in a bilingual marketplace and operates in both English and French.[[192]](#cite_note-daigle-195) The City of Ottawa provides services in English and French[[193]](#cite_note-196)[[194]](#cite_note-197)[[195]](#cite_note-198) and the [Ottawa–Gatineau](/wiki/National_Capital_Region_(Canada) "National Capital Region (Canada)") census metropolitan area contains a mix of anglophones and francophones.[[196]](#cite_note-CMAProfile-199) Longstanding Senators policy calls for providing services and marketing in both English and French to its bilingual fanbase. A bilingual version of the [Canadian national anthem](/wiki/Canadian_national_anthem "Canadian national anthem") is sung before home games, and all announcements are in both languages. It has been estimated that 40 percent of season ticket holders are francophone.[[197]](#cite_note-200) Senators games are broadcast on both the English-language TSN and the French-language RDS networks, in a long-standing agreement with Bell Media.[[198]](#cite_note-201)

### Logo and jersey design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Logo and jersey design")]

The team colours are black, red and white with gold trim.[[199]](#cite_note-enc-202) Except for the gold, the colours match the colours of the original Senators. The team's home jersey is black with red trim, while the away jersey is white with black and red trim. The club's [logo](/wiki/Logo "Logo") is the head of a [Roman general](/wiki/Legatus "Legatus"), a member of the [Senate](/wiki/Roman_Senate "Roman Senate") of the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") in a gold semi-circle.[[200]](#cite_note-scanlan_may_91-203) The original logo, unveiled on May 23, 1991, described the general as a "[centurion](/wiki/Centurion "Centurion") figure, strong and prominent" according to its designer, Tony Milchard.[[200]](#cite_note-scanlan_may_91-203) The logo was criticized by fans, and the designer hoped that people would "grow to like it".[[201]](#cite_note-204)

The Senators jersey has an 'S' winged shoulder crest, with 'Est. 1894 (in Roman numerals)', the year (in error) believed at the time (1991) to be the year of the establishment of the original Senators.[[200]](#cite_note-scanlan_may_91-203) The original Senators were actually founded in 1883.[[202]](#cite_note-205) This 'S' secondary logo was used from the 1992-93 season through 2006-2007 and was reintroduced in 2020 with slightly different coloration and continues to be used.[[203]](#cite_note-206)

From 1992 to 1995, the Senators' primary road jerseys were black with red stripes. The numbers were red for the first season but switched to white afterward. White stripes were added to the uniform in 1995. The white uniforms, worn on home games until 2003 and on road games until 2007, featured black sleeves, tail stripes with red accents, and black lettering.[[204]](#cite_note-SensJerseyHistory-207) In 1997, the Senators unveiled a red third jersey. It featured the first iteration of the "forward-facing" centurion logo, designed by Kevin Caradonna, head of the team's graphic design department, who also designed the mascot "[Spartacat](/wiki/Spartacat "Spartacat")."[[205]](#cite_note-208) The jersey became the team's primary dark jersey starting in 1999. From 2000 to 2007, the Senators also wore a black alternate jersey with gold, red and white accents.[[204]](#cite_note-SensJerseyHistory-207)

[![head of soldier wearing helmet](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/db/Ottawa_Senators.svg/250px-Ottawa_Senators.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ottawa_Senators.svg)

The Senators' primary logo from [2007–08](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_NHL_season "2007–08 NHL season") until [2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_NHL_season "2019–20 NHL season")

A new jersey design was unveiled on August 22, 2007, in conjunction with the league-wide adoption of the *[Rbk EDGE](/wiki/Rbk_EDGE "Rbk EDGE")* jerseys by [Reebok](/wiki/Reebok "Reebok") for the 2007–08 season.[[206]](#cite_note-update-logo-209) The jersey incorporates the original Senators' 'O' logo as a shoulder patch. At the same time, the team updated its logos and switched its usage. The primary logo, which, according to team owner Eugene Melnyk, "represents strength and determination" is an update of the old secondary logo.[[207]](#cite_note-210)

Before the 2008–09 season, the Senators unveiled a new black third jersey featuring the shortened "SENS" moniker in front. The centurion logo adorns the shoulders, and the striping was inspired by the team's original black jerseys.[[204]](#cite_note-SensJerseyHistory-207)

In 2011, the Senators introduced a throwback-inspired third jersey design. Predominantly black, the jersey incorporated horizontal striping intended to be reminiscent of the original Senators' 'barber-pole' designs. Shield-type patches were added to the shoulders. The design of the shield-type patches was meant to be similar to the shield patches that the original Senators added to their jerseys after each Stanley Cup championship win. The patches spell the team name, one in English and one in French. The design was a collaborative effort between the Senators and a fan in Gatineau, Quebec, who had been circulating a version of it on the internet since 2009.[[208]](#cite_note-211) The black third jerseys served as the basis of the Senators' [2014 Heritage Classic](/wiki/2014_Heritage_Classic "2014 Heritage Classic") jerseys, which used cream as the base colour.[[204]](#cite_note-SensJerseyHistory-207)

In 2017, the Senators' jerseys received a slight makeover when [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") replaced Reebok as the NHL's uniform provider. The number font was changed to match those of their recent third jerseys, which were retired after the 2016–17 season. Before the 2018–19 season, the Senators brought back the red jerseys worn during the NHL 100 Classic as a third jersey. The design featured a silver "O" in front with black trim amid horizontal black, silver and white stripes.[[204]](#cite_note-SensJerseyHistory-207)

In 2020, the Senators reintroduced its 1997–2007 logo with the jersey set used from 1992 to 1995.[[209]](#cite_note-212) The updated logo uses a gold outline as opposed to red. The new uniforms, while largely resembling the originals from the 1990s, retained the lettering font used since the Adidas takeover. In contrast, the white uniforms retained only the black and red stripes along the upper arm sleeves. Both the home and away uniforms include a red band across the very bottom of the jerseys.[[210]](#cite_note-213)[[211]](#cite_note-214) In addition, the Senators unveiled a "Reverse Retro" alternate uniform; the design was of the original 1992–93 uniform but with red as the base colour.[[212]](#cite_note-215) In the 2022–23 season, the Senators wore "Reverse Retro" uniforms based on the alternates they wore from 1997 to 2007, but with the current 2-D logo in front, black as the base colour and less white elements.[[213]](#cite_note-216)

Prior to the 2025–26 season, the Senators unveiled a red alternate uniform, again using the primary logo as the crest along with gold and black stripes along the waist, shoulders and sleeves.[[214]](#cite_note-217)

### Arena entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Arena entertainment")]

[![person wearing a lion outfit and hockey jersey](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Ottawa_Winterlude_Festival_%2834757903963%29.jpg/250px-Ottawa_Winterlude_Festival_%2834757903963%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ottawa_Winterlude_Festival_(34757903963).jpg)

[Spartacat](/wiki/Spartacat "Spartacat") is the official mascot for the Ottawa Senators.

At many home games, the fans are entertained both outside and inside the Canadian Tire Centre with myriad entertainers – live music, DJs, giveaways and promotions. The live music includes the traditional Scottish music of the 'Sons of Scotland Pipe Band' of Ottawa, along with highland dancers.[[215]](#cite_note-218) Before and during games, entertainment is hosted by Spartacat, the official mascot of the Senators, an [anthropomorphic](/wiki/Anthropomorphic "Anthropomorphic") lion. He made his debut on the Senators' opening night: October 8, 1992.[[216]](#cite_note-219) During intermissions, the entertainment varies with on-ice contests, youth games, t-shirt giveaways, live bands and DJs. At each game, a selected fan rides one of the on-ice resurfacers ("Zambonis"). The team's public address announcer is Jonathan Trottier and their in-game DJ is Alexis Marchand.[[217]](#cite_note-220) When the Senators score a goal, the team's goal horn is a Nathan Airchime M3H from a retired [VIA Rail](/wiki/VIA_Rail "VIA Rail") train which was initially used in the Ottawa Civic Centre, and the team's goal song is "[Song 2](/wiki/Song_2 "Song 2")" by [Blur](/wiki/Blur_(band) "Blur (band)").[[218]](#cite_note-221) At each game, the Senators spotlight a Canadian veteran soldier.
