Like other NHL arenas in Canada, *[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")* is sung before the opening faceoff, along with *[The Star-Spangled Banner](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner")* if an American team is visiting. *O Canada* is sung in both English and French with the first half of the first stanza and chorus sung in English and the second half of the first stanza sung in French.[[219]](#cite_note-222) From 1994 until 2016, the national anthems were sung by former [Ontario Provincial Police](/wiki/Ontario_Provincial_Police "Ontario Provincial Police") Constable [Lyndon Slewidge](/wiki/Lyndon_Slewidge "Lyndon Slewidge").[[220]](#cite_note-223)[[221]](#cite_note-224) Currently, the team has no regular singer. During *O Canada*, a large Canadian flag is unfurled and passed from fan to fan in the lower bowl section.

The Senators have their own theme song titled *Trumpeters Cry,* which is played as the team comes on the ice and is also used in Sens TV web videos.[[222]](#cite_note-225) The song was written by Ottawa singer-songwriter Andres del Castillo, who was formerly of the band [Eight Seconds](/wiki/Eight_Seconds "Eight Seconds").[[223]](#cite_note-im230208-226)

### Attendance, revenue and ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Attendance, revenue and ownership")]

On April 18, 2008, the club announced its final attendance figures for 2007–08. The club had 40 sell-outs out of 41 home dates, a total attendance of 812,665 during the regular season, placing the club third in attendance in the NHL.[[224]](#cite_note-2008-wrap-227) The number of sell-outs and the total attendance were both club records. The previous attendance records were set during the 2005–06 with a season total of 798,453 and 33 sell-outs.[[225]](#cite_note-media-guide-pg170-228) In the 2006–07 regular season, total attendance was 794,271, with 31 sell-outs out of 41 home dates or an average attendance of 19,372. In the 2007 playoffs, the Senators played nine games with nine sell-outs and an attendance of 181,272 for an average of 20,141, the highest in team history.[[225]](#cite_note-media-guide-pg170-228) Until recent seasons, the club was regularly represented in the top half in attendance in the NHL.[[226]](#cite_note-229) In 2018–19, the Senators average attendance was 14,553, 27th in the league.[[227]](#cite_note-230) Attendance dropped further in the 2019–20 season, dropping to an average of 12,618, the lowest in the league.[[228]](#cite_note-cbc-2020-12-11-231)

In 2022, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes_(magazine) "Forbes (magazine)")* magazine valued the Senators at US$800 million.[[229]](#cite_note-232) *Forbes* estimated the debt/value ratio at 25% and that the team earned US$47 million in 2020–21 on revenue of US$157 million.[[230]](#cite_note-forbes-dec22-233) The team is now owned by Michael Andlauer who is the majority owner along with his partners, Eugene Melnyk's daughters, and a group of Canadian businessmen.[[142]](#cite_note-Michael_Andlauer_is_the_new_owner_of_the_Ottawa_Senators-145) It was previously owned by Eugene Melynk who bought the team and arena out of bankruptcy in 2003 for US$92 million.[[231]](#cite_note-forbes-melnyk-234) Before Melnyk's ownership, the Senators were owned by founder Terrace Investments, majority owned by Rod Bryden, until it declared bankruptcy in 2002.[[16]](#cite_note-MacGregor1993-cit-19)[[30]](#cite_note-whig-33) Terrace Investments initially won the bid for an NHL franchise in 1990.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFinnigan1992201-9)

### Sens Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Sens Army")]

[![group of people outdoors at night on a city street](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Sensmile.jpg/250px-Sensmile.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sensmile.jpg)

Sens Mile on [Elgin Street](/wiki/Elgin_Street_(Ottawa) "Elgin Street (Ottawa)") during the [2007 Stanley Cup playoffs](/wiki/2007_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2007 Stanley Cup playoffs")

The fans of the Senators are known as the *Sens Army*.[[232]](#cite_note-235) Like most hockey fanatics, they are known to dress up for games, some in [Roman legionary](/wiki/Roman_legion "Roman legion") clothing. For the 2006–07 playoff run, more fans than ever before would wear red. Fan activities included 'Red Rallies' of decorated cars, fan rallies at Ottawa City Hall Plaza and the 'Sens Mile' along Elgin Street, where fans would congregate.[[233]](#cite_note-236)

#### Sens Mile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Sens Mile")]

Much like the [Red Mile](/wiki/Red_Mile "Red Mile") in [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary") during the [Flames](/wiki/Calgary_Flames "Calgary Flames")' 2004 Stanley Cup run and the Copper Kilometre in [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton") during the Oilers' 2006 Stanley Cup run, Senators' fans took to the streets to celebrate their team's success during the 2007 playoffs. The idea to have a 'Sens Mile' on downtown [Elgin Street](/wiki/Elgin_Street_(Ottawa) "Elgin Street (Ottawa)"), a street with numerous restaurants and pubs, began as a grassroots campaign on [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") before game four of the Ottawa–Buffalo conference finals series.[[234]](#cite_note-237) After the game five win, Ottawa residents closed the street to traffic for a spontaneous celebration.[[235]](#cite_note-238) The City of Ottawa then closed Elgin Street for each game of the Stanley Cup Final.[[236]](#cite_note-239)

### Team records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Team records")]

Main article: [List of Ottawa Senators records](/wiki/List_of_Ottawa_Senators_records "List of Ottawa Senators records")

[![man wearing helmet](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Dany_Heatley.jpg/250px-Dany_Heatley.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dany_Heatley.jpg)

[Dany Heatley](/wiki/Dany_Heatley "Dany Heatley") holds the franchise record for most goals in a season, scoring 50 goals in the [2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_NHL_season "2005–06 NHL season") and [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_NHL_season "2006–07 NHL season") seasons.

* Most goals in a season – [Dany Heatley](/wiki/Dany_Heatley "Dany Heatley"), 50 (2005–06, 2006–07)
* Most goals in a season, defenceman – [Erik Karlsson](/wiki/Erik_Karlsson "Erik Karlsson"), 21 (2014–15)
* Most assists in a season – [Jason Spezza](/wiki/Jason_Spezza "Jason Spezza"), 71 (2005–06)
* Most assists in a season, defenceman – [Erik Karlsson](/wiki/Erik_Karlsson "Erik Karlsson"), 66 (2015–16)
* Most points in a season – [Dany Heatley](/wiki/Dany_Heatley "Dany Heatley"), 105 (2006–07)
* Most points in a season, defenceman – [Erik Karlsson](/wiki/Erik_Karlsson "Erik Karlsson"), 82 (2015–16)
* Most points in a season, rookie – [Alexei Yashin](/wiki/Alexei_Yashin "Alexei Yashin"), 79 (1993–94)
* Most penalty minutes in a season – [Mike Peluso](/wiki/Mike_Peluso_(ice_hockey,_born_1965) "Mike Peluso (ice hockey, born 1965)"), 318 (1992–93)
* Highest +/– rating in a season – [Daniel Alfredsson](/wiki/Daniel_Alfredsson "Daniel Alfredsson"), +42 (2006–07)
* Most games played – [Chris Phillips](/wiki/Chris_Phillips "Chris Phillips"), 1,179 (up to 2022–23 season)
* Most playoff games played – [Daniel Alfredsson](/wiki/Daniel_Alfredsson "Daniel Alfredsson"), 121 (1997–2013)
* Most goaltender wins in a season – [Patrick Lalime](/wiki/Patrick_Lalime "Patrick Lalime"), 39 (2002–2003)
* Most shutouts in a season – [Patrick Lalime](/wiki/Patrick_Lalime "Patrick Lalime"), 8 (2002–03)
* Lowest [GAA](/wiki/Goals_against_average "Goals against average") in a season – [Craig Anderson](/wiki/Craig_Anderson_(ice_hockey) "Craig Anderson (ice hockey)"), 1.69 (2012–13)
* Best save percentage in a season – [Craig Anderson](/wiki/Craig_Anderson_(ice_hockey) "Craig Anderson (ice hockey)"), .941 (2012–13)

**Source:** Ottawa Senators.[[283]](#cite_note-Ottawa_Senators_staff_2010_190–191-287)

## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

Ottawa Senators games are broadcast locally in both the English and French languages. As of the 2014–15 season, regional television rights to the Senators' regular season games not broadcast nationally by [Sportsnet](/wiki/Sportsnet "Sportsnet"), [TVA Sports](/wiki/TVA_Sports "TVA Sports"), or *[Hockey Night in Canada](/wiki/Hockey_Night_in_Canada "Hockey Night in Canada")* are owned by [Bell Media](/wiki/Bell_Media "Bell Media") under a 12-year contract, with games airing in English on [TSN5](/wiki/TSN5 "TSN5"), and in French on [RDS](/wiki/R%C3%A9seau_des_sports "Réseau des sports"). Regional broadcasts are available within the team's designated region (shared with the Montreal Canadiens), which includes the Ottawa River valley, Eastern Ontario (portions are shared with the Toronto Maple Leafs), along with [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), the [Maritime provinces](/wiki/Maritimes "Maritimes") and [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador").[[284]](#cite_note-tsn-regionalNHL-288)[[285]](#cite_note-tsn-newsenscontract-289)[[286]](#cite_note-sun-tsnsens-290)

[![outline map of Canada showing Senators broadcast regions in Ontario, Quebec and the Maritimes](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/TSN_Regional_Feeds.svg/500px-TSN_Regional_Feeds.svg.png)](/wiki/File:TSN_Regional_Feeds.svg)

The Ottawa Senators broadcast area in blue and green

On radio, all home and away games are broadcast on a five-station network stretching across Eastern Ontario, including one American station, [WQTK](/wiki/WQTK "WQTK") in [Ogdensburg, New York](/wiki/Ogdensburg,_New_York "Ogdensburg, New York").[[287]](#cite_note-sens-broadcast-partners-291) The flagship radio station is [CFGO](/wiki/CFGO "CFGO") in Ottawa.[[287]](#cite_note-sens-broadcast-partners-291) Radio broadcasts on CFGO began in 1997–98; the contract has since been extended through the 2025–2026 as part of Bell Media's rights deal with the team.[[285]](#cite_note-tsn-newsenscontract-289)[[288]](#cite_note-292) The Senators are broadcast on radio in French through Intersport Production and [CJFO-FM](/wiki/CJFO-FM "CJFO-FM") in Ottawa.[[289]](#cite_note-293) Nicolas St. Pierre provides play-by-play, with Alain Sanscartier as colour commentator.[[290]](#cite_note-294)

[Sportsnet East](/wiki/Sportsnet_East "Sportsnet East") held English regional rights to the Sens before the 2014–15 season. In April 2014, [Dean Brown](/wiki/Dean_Brown_(sportscaster) "Dean Brown (sportscaster)"), who had called play-by-play for Senators games since the team's inception, stated that it was "extremely unlikely" that he would move to TSN and continue his role. He noted that the network already had four commentators among its personalities – including [Gord Miller](/wiki/Gord_Miller_(sportscaster) "Gord Miller (sportscaster)"), [Chris Cuthbert](/wiki/Chris_Cuthbert "Chris Cuthbert"), [Rod Black](/wiki/Rod_Black "Rod Black"), and [Paul Romanuk](/wiki/Paul_Romanuk "Paul Romanuk") (who was, however, picked up by Rogers for its national NHL coverage in June 2014), who were likely candidates to serve as the new voices of the Senators.[[291]](#cite_note-sn-fullteam-295)[[292]](#cite_note-ottawasun-deanbrown-296) Brown ultimately moved to the Senators' radio broadcasts alongside Gord Wilson.[[293]](#cite_note-cfra-brownwilson-297) Both Miller and Cuthbert, along with [Ray Ferraro](/wiki/Ray_Ferraro "Ray Ferraro") and [Jamie McLennan](/wiki/Jamie_McLennan "Jamie McLennan"), became the Senators' TV voices on TSN from 2014 to 2020. After Cuthbert joined Sportsnet in 2020, former Canucks radio voice Jon Abbott took over as the secondary play-by-play commentator in games where Miller is assigned to call the Maple Leafs. [Mike Johnson](/wiki/Mike_Johnson_(ice_hockey) "Mike Johnson (ice hockey)"), who concurrently works colour commentary for the Canadiens and Maple Leafs on TSN, replaced Ferraro as an alternate to McLennan.[[294]](#cite_note-tsnsens2021-298) After Abbott left to join the Calgary Flames in 2024, broadcaster Matt Cullen and Kenzie Lalonde split time as the backup to Miller.[[295]](#cite_note-299)[[296]](#cite_note-300)

During the 2006–07 and 2007–08 seasons, several games were only available in video on [pay-per-view](/wiki/Pay-per-view "Pay-per-view") or at local movie theatres in the Ottawa area.[[297]](#cite_note-301) The "Sens TV" service was suspended indefinitely as of September 24, 2008.[[298]](#cite_note-302) In 2010, Sportsnet launched a secondary channel for selected Senators games as part of its [Sportsnet One](/wiki/Sportsnet_One "Sportsnet One") service.[[299]](#cite_note-303) Selected Senators games were broadcast in French by RDS and TVA Sports. On the RDS network, Felix Seguin and former Senators goaltender Patrick Lalime[[300]](#cite_note-rds-lalime-304) were the announcers from the 2011–12 season to the 2013–14 season, and Michel Y. Lacroix and Norman Flynn starting in the 2014–15 season. The TVA Sports broadcast team consisted of Michel Langevin, [Yvon Pedneault](/wiki/Yvon_Pedneault "Yvon Pedneault") and [Enrico Ciccone](/wiki/Enrico_Ciccone "Enrico Ciccone").[[301]](#cite_note-305)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ottawa_Senators&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** *NHL Media Guide 2010*. The [original Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators_(original) "Ottawa Senators (original)") organization, also known as the Ottawa Hockey Club, won the Stanley Cup 11 times, not the current franchise founded in 1992. Neither the NHL nor the Senators claim that the current Senators are a continuation of the original organization or franchise. The awards, statistics and championships of both eras are kept separate, and the NHL franchise founding date of the current Senators is in 1992.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** French: *Club de hockey Les Sénateurs d'Ottawa*.[[4]](#cite_note-5)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** The [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") counts 11. The [Hockey Hall of Fame](/wiki/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame "Hockey Hall of Fame") count is 10.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-Finnigan_261-0)** Finnigan was honoured for his play from 1923 through 1934 for the original Ottawa Senators (playing right wing, 1923–1931 and 1932–1934). He was the last surviving Senator from the Stanley Cup winners of 1927 and participated in the 'Bring Back the Senators' campaign.
